title: ITR Filing Deadlines for AY 2026-27 — Key Dates You Must Know
author: Sivakumar Chandrasekar
excerpt: The Income Tax Return filing season for AY 2026-27 (FY 2025-26) is open. Here are every deadline you need to mark on your calendar — missing them can cost you up to ₹5,000 in penalties.
body:
The Income Tax Return filing season for Assessment Year 2026-27 (Financial Year 2025-26) is now open. The Income Tax Department has notified the updated ITR forms and the e-filing portal is accepting returns. Here is a complete calendar of every deadline you need to track.

Key ITR Filing Deadlines

  • 31 July 2026 — Individuals, HUFs, BOI, and non-audit cases (ITR-1, ITR-2, ITR-3, ITR-4). This covers salaried employees, pensioners, and small business owners not subject to tax audit.
  • 31 October 2026 — All taxpayers whose accounts are required to be audited under the Income-tax Act or any other law (businesses and professionals with turnover above ₹1 crore / ₹50 lakh respectively).
  • 30 November 2026 — Taxpayers who are required to furnish a report in respect of international or specified domestic transactions (Transfer Pricing cases).
  • 31 December 2026 — Belated return under Section 139(4). A late fee of ₹5,000 (₹1,000 if total income does not exceed ₹5 lakh) applies under Section 234F, plus interest under Section 234A.
  • 31 March 2027 — Revised return under Section 139(5) to correct any omission or wrong statement in an earlier filed return.

Updated Return (ITR-U)

If you missed filing altogether for a past year, you can still file an Updated Return (ITR-U) under Section 139(8A) within four years from the end of the relevant assessment year — i.e., up to 31 March 2031 for AY 2026-27. However, ITR-U requires you to pay an additional tax of 25%–50% of the tax and interest due, depending on when you file.

Who Must File ITR for FY 2025-26?

  • Individuals with gross total income exceeding the basic exemption limit (₹2.5 lakh under old regime / ₹3 lakh under new regime)
  • Those with foreign assets or foreign income
  • Those with deposits exceeding ₹1 crore in current accounts
  • Those with electricity expenditure exceeding ₹1 lakh in a year
  • Those with foreign travel expenditure exceeding ₹2 lakh
  • All companies and firms (regardless of income)

New Tax Regime — Default for FY 2025-26

Remember that the New Tax Regime is the default for AY 2026-27. If you wish to opt for the Old Tax Regime (to claim deductions like 80C, HRA, LTA, etc.), you must explicitly do so while filing your return. Salaried individuals can switch every year; business/professionals can opt out only once.

Documents Checklist

  • Form 16 from employer (Part A + Part B)
  • Form 26AS and Annual Information Statement (AIS) from the IT portal
  • Bank statements for all accounts
  • Interest certificates from banks and post offices
  • Investment proofs (if opting for old regime)
  • Capital gains statements from brokers
  • Rental income receipts and home loan certificates

Filing your return early avoids last-minute portal congestion and gives you time to respond to any notices. Reach out to MyTaxPert to file accurately and maximise your refund or minimise your liability.

title: New Income Tax Bill 2025: What Changes for Every Taxpayer
author: Sivakumar Chandrasekar
excerpt: The Income Tax Bill 2025, introduced in Parliament in February 2025, aims to replace the 60-year-old Income Tax Act of 1961. Here is what the key changes mean for you.
body:
The Income Tax Bill 2025 was introduced in the Lok Sabha on 13 February 2025, with the stated goal of making India's direct tax law simpler, clearer, and shorter. The existing Income Tax Act, 1961 — which has grown to over 500 sections and thousands of sub-sections over 60 years — is being replaced with a more streamlined statute.

Key Changes at a Glance

1. 'Tax Year' Replaces 'Previous Year / Assessment Year'

One of the most confusing aspects of the old Act was the two-year terminology: Previous Year (the year you earn income) and Assessment Year (the year you file). The new Bill introduces a single term — 'Tax Year' — which aligns with the financial year (April–March). This removes significant confusion for taxpayers.

2. Simplified Language

Complex legal provisions have been rewritten in plain English with tables and formulas. Provisos and explanations that were buried inside long sections are now clearly laid out as separate rules, making the law much easier to read without a professional interpreter.

3. Faceless Assessment — Strengthened

The faceless assessment regime, introduced in 2020, is now given a stronger legislative footing. All scrutiny assessments, appeals, and penalty proceedings must be conducted without any human interface between the taxpayer and the assessing officer.

4. No Change to Tax Rates

Importantly, the Bill does not change tax rates or slabs — those are announced via the Finance Bill each year. The new Income Tax Code is purely a structural reform of the law, not a tax-rate change.

5. Residency Rules Clarified

The definition of Resident and Ordinarily Resident (ROR), Resident but Not Ordinarily Resident (RNOR), and Non-Resident (NR) has been simplified. The 182-day and 60-day tests are now presented in a single table, making it easier for NRIs to determine their tax status.

6. Capital Gains — Cleaner Structure

Long-Term and Short-Term Capital Gains provisions are reorganised into a single chapter with clear tables showing holding periods and applicable rates for different asset classes — equities, property, gold, mutual funds, etc.

7. TDS and TCS — Rationalised

Multiple TDS provisions with overlapping thresholds have been consolidated. The Bill also proposes higher TDS rates for non-filers of ITR — an incentive to file returns on time.

When Does It Take Effect?

The Bill was referred to a Parliamentary Select Committee after introduction. Subject to committee recommendations and Parliament's approval, it is expected to take effect from 1 April 2026 (i.e., from Tax Year 2026-27). Until then, the Income Tax Act 1961 continues to apply.

What Should You Do?

For FY 2025-26 returns (filed in 2026), the old Act applies. However, it is a good time to review your finances, clean up your investments, and plan your tax strategy for the new regime. MyTaxPert can help you navigate both regimes and plan ahead for the transition.

title: GST Compliance Calendar FY 2025-26: Every Due Date in One Place
author: Sivakumar Chandrasekar
excerpt: GST compliance requires businesses to file multiple returns every month and year. This is your complete calendar for FY 2025-26 — GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, GSTR-9, and everything in between.
body:
Staying GST-compliant means keeping track of multiple return types, their filing frequencies, and their due dates. Missing a GST deadline attracts late fees of ₹50 per day (₹25 CGST + ₹25 SGST) for returns with tax liability, and ₹20 per day for nil returns. Here is your complete compliance calendar for FY 2025-26.

Monthly Returns — Regular Taxpayers (Turnover > ₹5 Crore)

ReturnFrequencyDue Date
GSTR-1 (Outward supplies)Monthly11th of following month
IFF (Invoice Furnishing Facility)Monthly (QRMP)13th of following month
GSTR-3B (Summary + Tax payment)Monthly20th of following month

QRMP Scheme — Small Taxpayers (Turnover ≤ ₹5 Crore)

Under the Quarterly Return Monthly Payment (QRMP) scheme, eligible taxpayers file GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B quarterly but pay tax monthly via a fixed sum payment (Form GST PMT-06).

  • Monthly Tax Payment (PMT-06) — 25th of following month (for Months 1 and 2 of the quarter)
  • GSTR-1 (Quarterly) — 13th of month following the quarter
  • GSTR-3B (Quarterly) — 22nd or 24th of month following the quarter (depending on state category)

Annual Returns

  • GSTR-9 (Annual Return) — 31 December 2025 for FY 2024-25 (mandatory for turnover > ₹2 crore)
  • GSTR-9C (Reconciliation Statement) — 31 December 2025 for FY 2024-25 (mandatory for turnover > ₹5 crore)

Other Important GST Compliances

  • E-way Bill — Required for inter-state goods movement exceeding ₹50,000
  • E-Invoice — Mandatory for businesses with aggregate turnover exceeding ₹5 crore (since August 2023)
  • GST Audit — Internal audit and reconciliation recommended quarterly

Consequences of Non-Compliance

Apart from late fees, persistent non-compliance can lead to:

  • Suspension or cancellation of GST registration
  • Blocking of Input Tax Credit (ITC) for customers
  • Prosecution in cases of wilful evasion

MyTaxPert handles end-to-end GST compliance for businesses of all sizes — from monthly return filing to annual reconciliation and audit support. Contact us to discuss a compliance package tailored to your business.
